agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A new feature request introduces a 'confirmed' status for quotes, but the term 'confirmed' is already established in the order domain with a fundamentally different meaning. In orders, 'confirmed' means paid and immutable. In quotes, 'confirmed' would mean verbally agreed but unpaid and still mutable. This is a direct contradiction in the ubiquitous language.","bounded_context":"The feature spans two bounded contexts: Order Management (where 'confirmed' is already defined) and Quote Management (where 'confirmed' is being introduced with conflicting semantics).","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Confirmed Order","definition":"An order that has been paid and is queued for fulfillment. It is immutable — no further changes are permitted."},{"term":"Confirmed Quote (proposed)","definition":"A quote where the customer has verbally agreed to terms but payment has NOT occurred. Implicitly still mutable (e.g., payment could fail, customer could retract verbal agreement)."},{"term":"Quote","definition":"A pricing proposal presented to a customer by a sales agent, preceding an order."},{"term":"Order","definition":"A committed transaction that progresses through statuses from creation to fulfillment."},{"term":"Immutable","definition":"Cannot be changed once the status is reached. A core invariant of confirmed orders."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"A confirmed order has been paid and is queued for fulfillment.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is the existing, established definition. Violating it would corrupt order state — downstream fulfillment, accounting, and inventory all depend on 'confirmed' meaning 'paid'.","testable_assertion":"Every order in 'confirmed' status has a completed payment record associated with it."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"A confirmed order is immutable — no fields may be modified after confirmation.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Immutability after payment is a data integrity constraint. Fulfillment, invoicing, and audit trails depend on confirmed orders never changing.","testable_assertion":"Any attempt to modify a confirmed order returns an error and the order remains unchanged."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"A confirmed quote represents verbal customer agreement but does NOT imply payment.","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is a proposed business policy for the sales workflow. The threshold for what constitutes 'confirmation' of a quote could change (e.g., written agreement vs verbal).","testable_assertion":"A quote in 'confirmed' status has no associated payment record."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"The term 'confirmed' must have a single unambiguous meaning across the domain to prevent misinterpretation by developers, support staff, and automated systems.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Ubiquitous language consistency is a foundational DDD invariant. If the same term means different things in overlapping contexts, every query, report, dashboard, and API consumer is at risk of misinterpretation.","testable_assertion":"Searching the codebase and documentation for 'confirmed' returns entities that share the same semantic definition (paid + immutable OR a renamed alternative)."}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A quote exists in 'draft' or 'sent' status","when":"A sales agent records verbal customer agreement","then":"The quote transitions to a status that unambiguously indicates verbal agreement without payment (e.g., 'accepted', 'verbally_agreed', 'customer_approved')"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A quote has been verbally agreed upon","when":"The customer completes payment","then":"An order is created in 'confirmed' status, preserving the existing meaning of 'confirmed' as paid + immutable"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"The system uses the term 'confirmed' for orders","when":"Any new status is introduced for quotes","then":"The new status term is distinct from 'confirmed' to avoid semantic collision across bounded contexts"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"A quote is in the verbally-agreed status","when":"The customer retracts agreement or payment fails","then":"The quote can revert to a prior status, because unlike confirmed orders, verbal agreement does not imply immutability"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A developer writes a query filtering all 'confirmed' entities and inadvertently mixes confirmed orders (paid, immutable) with confirmed quotes (unpaid, mutable).","expected_behavior":"This must be impossible. Either the term is disambiguated, or bounded context separation prevents cross-entity queries from conflating the two."},{"scenario":"A support agent sees 'confirmed' on a quote and tells a customer their order is paid and being fulfilled.","expected_behavior":"The status label visible to support and customers must make it clear that payment has not occurred. 'Confirmed' is dangerously misleading in this context."},{"scenario":"A reporting dashboard counts 'confirmed' items as revenue-committed, including quotes that have no payment.","expected_behavior":"Revenue recognition must only include paid, confirmed orders. Quotes with verbal agreement are pipeline, not revenue."},{"scenario":"A verbally-agreed quote sits indefinitely without payment.","expected_behavior":"Business rules should define a timeout or follow-up workflow for quotes in verbal agreement status. This is a policy decision, not a system invariant."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"High risk of revenue misreporting if 'confirmed' quotes are conflated with 'confirmed' orders in financial systems. Verbal agreements are not revenue — treating them as such could violate revenue recognition standards.","operational":"Semantic collision will create confusion across engineering, support, sales, and reporting teams. Debugging and incident response become harder when the same term has two meanings.","user":"Sales agents need a clear status for verbal agreement — the business need is valid. But the proposed term will confuse support staff and customers who associate 'confirmed' with payment completed.","risk":"Domain language corruption is one of the highest-risk architectural mistakes. It compounds over time as more code, reports, and integrations are built on ambiguous terminology. The cost to fix increases exponentially.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Sales Agents","impact":"Positive — they gain a workflow status for verbal agreements. The exact label ('accepted' vs 'confirmed') has minimal impact on their workflow."},{"group":"Customer Support","impact":"High negative risk — 'confirmed' on a quote will be misread as 'paid' based on existing order semantics, leading to incorrect customer communications."},{"group":"Finance / Accounting","impact":"High negative risk — revenue reports that filter on 'confirmed' status could incorrectly include unpaid quotes."},{"group":"Engineering","impact":"Moderate negative — developers must reason about 'confirmed' differently depending on entity type, increasing cognitive load and bug surface area."},{"group":"Customers","impact":"Moderate negative risk — if customer-facing status shows 'confirmed', customers may believe they have a binding, paid commitment when they do not."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"The spec contains a direct contradiction: the established domain definition says 'confirmed' means paid + immutable, while the feature request uses 'confirmed' to mean unpaid + mutable. Both definitions are explicitly stated and cannot coexist under the same term. Confidence is low because the feature cannot ship as specified without violating the existing domain invariant.","high_confidence_areas":["The semantic collision between 'confirmed order' and 'confirmed quote' is unambiguous and real","The business need for a verbal-agreement status on quotes is valid","The term 'confirmed' for quotes will cause cross-domain confusion"],"low_confidence_areas":["What the preferred alternative term should be — this requires sales team input"],"assumptions":["The existing 'confirmed order' definition is authoritative and will not be changed","Orders and quotes share enough system surface area (reports, dashboards, APIs) that term collision will cause real problems"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The term 'confirmed' has an established meaning (paid + immutable for orders) that directly contradicts the proposed meaning (unpaid + mutable for quotes). Using the same term for both violates ubiquitous language consistency — a foundational domain invariant.","affected_stakeholders":["Sales Agents","Customer Support","Finance / Accounting","Engineering","Customers"],"options":["A) Rename the quote status to 'accepted' or 'verbally_agreed' to preserve the established meaning of 'confirmed'","B) Rename both statuses to be entity-qualified ('order_confirmed', 'quote_confirmed') — high migration cost, dilutes the existing term","C) Accept the collision and rely on entity type to disambiguate — high ongoing risk of misinterpretation"],"recommendation":"Option A. Rename the quote status to 'accepted'. It accurately describes the business event (customer accepted the terms), avoids collision with the established 'confirmed' definition, and requires zero changes to the existing order domain. The business need is fully met under a different, more precise label."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Reusing the term 'confirmed' for quotes with a meaning (unpaid, mutable) that directly contradicts the established definition (paid, immutable) for orders.","business_rule_broken":"BR-4: The term 'confirmed' must have a single unambiguous meaning across the domain."}]}
